instruments.name: Tischler Cervical Biopsy Forceps
instruments.description: A specialized gynecological instrument used to take punch biopsies of cervical tissue during a colposcopy.
instruments.detailedDescription: The Tischler cervical biopsy forceps features a distinctive bite block design that allows for the precise removal of a small, rectangular tissue specimen from the cervix. The jaws are designed to minimize tissue trauma while ensuring a clean cut, typically procuring a sample measuring approximately 6 x 3 x 1.5 mm. The instrument has a long, slender shaft to provide clear visualization through the vaginal speculum without obstructing the surgeon's line of sight.
instruments.uses: 🩸 Procurement of sharply dissected cervical tissue samples for histopathological examination to diagnose dysplasia or malignancy.
instruments.parts:
🔹 Ring handle
🔹 Long, slender shaft
🔹 Upper and lower jaws (bite block and cup)
🔹 Locking or spring mechanism
instruments.mechanism: ⚙️ The jaws interdigitate to sharply cut and enclose the tissue within the cup-like lower jaw, protecting the structural integrity of the specimen as the instrument is withdrawn from the vaginal canal.
instruments.commonProcedures:
🩺 Colposcopy
🩺 Cervical punch biopsy
🩺 General gynecological diagnostics
instruments.clinicalTip: 💡 Always handle the obtained specimen gently with a fine needle or atraumatic forceps. Squeezing the sample can cause "crush artifact," which severely compromises the pathologist's ability to evaluate the tissue.

instruments.name: Baby Tischler Biopsy Forceps
instruments.description: A smaller, more delicate version of the standard Tischler forceps used to obtain micro-specimens of cervical tissue.
instruments.detailedDescription: The Baby Tischler biopsy forceps is structurally identical to the standard Tischler but features a significantly smaller jaw size. It is designed to take a conservative bite of tissue, typically measuring around 4 x 2 x 1.5 mm or less. This instrument is ideal for patients with a smaller or stenotic cervix, or when multiple biopsy samples are required from different quadrants. The smaller bite size results in less bleeding and reduced patient discomfort.
instruments.uses: 🩸 Obtaining minimal tissue samples from the exocervix with reduced trauma, particularly in nulliparous women (women who have not given birth).
instruments.parts:
🔹 Ring handles
🔹 Long shaft
🔹 Miniaturized bite block jaws
🔹 Spring mechanism
instruments.mechanism: ⚙️ Operates with a guillotine-style punch action where a smaller cup jaw shears against an upper jaw to capture and hold a micro-specimen cleanly.
instruments.commonProcedures:
🩺 Colposcopy
🩺 Directed cervical biopsies
🩺 Cervical mapping
instruments.clinicalTip: 💡 This is the instrument of choice when obtaining multiple small biopsies during a single colposcopic exam, as the smaller tissue defects minimize bleeding and the need for hemostatic agents like Monsel's solution.

instruments.name: Kevorkian Biopsy Forceps
instruments.description: A heavy-duty, punch-style biopsy forceps featuring narrow, rectangular jaws with teeth, used primarily for dense cervical and endocervical biopsies.
instruments.detailedDescription: The Kevorkian biopsy forceps (sometimes called Kevorkian-Younge) is distinguished by its strong, narrow jaws that typically feature teeth on the lower jaw to grasp tissue securely before cutting. The bite produces a deep, rectangular tissue specimen. It features a heavy, durable construction with ring handles and a long shaft, allowing the surgeon to grasp and cut through dense, fibrotic, or slippery cervical tissue effectively.
instruments.uses: 🩸 Sampling of tough or dense cervical and lower endocervical tissue where standard smooth-jawed forceps might slip or fail to penetrate.
instruments.parts:
🔹 Ring handle
🔹 Heavy-duty shank
🔹 Rectangular biting jaws
🔹 Teeth on the grasping jaw (often alligator-style)
instruments.mechanism: ⚙️ The teeth anchor firmly into the cervical tissue to prevent slipping off the target site, while the closing action punches out a deep, rectangular block of tissue.
instruments.commonProcedures:
🩺 Colposcopy
🩺 Cervical punch biopsy
🩺 Endocervical tissue sampling
instruments.clinicalTip: 💡 Because this forceps takes a deeper bite and features anchoring teeth, inform the awake patient that they may feel a sharp pinch. Ensure patient stability, as this can be more uncomfortable than smaller, toothless punch forceps.

instruments.name: Schubert Biopsy Forceps
instruments.description: A sturdy gynecological instrument with angled jaws used to obtain tissue biopsies from anatomically difficult areas of the cervix.
instruments.detailedDescription: The Schubert biopsy forceps features a distinctively angled or curved tip that facilitates reaching awkward or recessed areas of the cervix. The jaws are designed to punch or bite into the tissue, retrieving a clean sample for histological analysis. It incorporates a solid, ring-handled design that allows the surgeon to apply significant mechanical force, making it highly effective for taking specimens from hardened or fibrotic lesions.
instruments.uses: 🩸 Excision of cervical tissue for diagnostic biopsy, specifically engineered for reaching awkwardly placed or obscure lesions.
instruments.parts:
🔹 Ring handle
🔹 Angled or curved distal shaft
🔹 Biting / punch jaws
🔹 Box lock joint
instruments.mechanism: ⚙️ The angled shaft allows for precise perpendicular positioning against the cervix, and the lever-action handle provides the mechanical advantage necessary to shear a firm tissue sample.
instruments.commonProcedures:
🩺 Cervical biopsy
🩺 Colposcopy
🩺 Gynecological diagnostics
instruments.clinicalTip: 💡 The angled design is highly advantageous for sampling lesions located on the posterior lip of the cervix, where a straight forceps would force the surgeon's hand to block their own line of sight.

instruments.name: Gaylor Uterine Biopsy Forceps
instruments.description: A curved biopsy forceps with circular, cup-like jaws used to obtain sharply dissected, shallow tissue samples from the cervix.
instruments.detailedDescription: The Gaylor uterine biopsy forceps is easily identifiable by its distinctive round, cup-shaped jaws and curved shanks. It incorporates a ring handle and often features a box-lock joint for stability during tissue extraction. The instrument is specifically designed to take a shallow, circular bite of tissue rather than a deep rectangular one. Its curved design helps the operator maneuver safely around the vaginal speculum.
instruments.uses: 🩸 Procurement of sharply dissected, superficial tissue specimens from the cervix or lower uterine segment.
instruments.parts:
🔹 Ring handles
🔹 Box lock joint
🔹 Curved shanks
🔹 Round / circular cutting jaws
instruments.mechanism: ⚙️ The round cutting jaws close tightly together in a punch-like mechanism to cleanly sever a small, superficial circular dome of tissue.
instruments.commonProcedures:
🩺 Cervical biopsy
🩺 Colposcopy
🩺 Uterine diagnostics
instruments.clinicalTip: 💡 Because the jaws are circular and take a wider, shallower bite, this instrument is excellent for sampling broad, flat lesions on the exocervix where deep penetration is not clinically necessary.

instruments.name: Keyes Dermal Punch
instruments.description: A small, tubular cutting instrument used to punch out full-thickness, circular skin specimens for dermatological biopsy.
instruments.detailedDescription: The Keyes dermal punch features a sharp, circular, hollow blade attached to a solid handle. Unlike jawed forceps, it is used by applying downward pressure and rotating the blade into the tissue to core out a cylindrical sample. The cutting edge is available in various diameters, typically ranging from 2mm to 8mm. It yields a uniform, full-thickness specimen that includes the epidermis, dermis, and sometimes underlying subcutaneous fat.
instruments.uses: 🩸 Obtaining full-thickness skin biopsies for the diagnosis of dermatological conditions, or the complete removal of small cutaneous lesions.
instruments.parts:
🔹 Solid handle (often ribbed for grip)
🔹 Hollow circular blade / coring tip
🔹 Sharp beveled cutting edge
instruments.mechanism: ⚙️ The sharp, hollow cylinder is pressed against the skin and rotated (twirled) back and forth to cut a perfect core of tissue. The core is then lifted with fine forceps and snipped at the base with scissors.
instruments.commonProcedures:
🩺 Skin biopsy (punch biopsy)
🩺 Excision of small nevi (moles)
🩺 Dermatological diagnostics
instruments.clinicalTip: 💡 Stretch the skin perpendicular to the natural skin tension lines (Langer's lines) before punching. After the core is removed and the skin relaxes, the circular defect will turn into an elliptical shape, making it much easier to close with sutures with minimal scarring.

instruments.name: Liston Bone Cutting Forceps
instruments.description: A heavy-duty orthopedic instrument used for cutting through thick, dense bones during surgical amputations and orthopedic procedures.
instruments.detailedDescription: The Liston bone cutting forceps is designed with stout, heavy blades and sharp, opposing inner edges to deliver maximum cutting force. It features a plier-like handle, often with a single or double-leaf spring mechanism, to reduce hand fatigue and ensure smooth reopening after each cut. It is available in straight or angled profiles to accommodate different surgical angles and access points.
instruments.uses: 🩸 Used for resecting, dividing, or trimming thick, tough bones such as long bones, digits, or ribs during major orthopedic surgeries.
instruments.parts:
🔹 Plier-style handles
🔹 Double-leaf spring mechanism
🔹 Box lock or heavy screw joint
🔹 Sharp, heavy-duty cutting jaws
instruments.mechanism: ⚙️ The instrument acts as a robust first-class lever, multiplying the force exerted by the surgeon's grip to shear smoothly through dense cortical bone without splintering it.
instruments.commonProcedures:
🩺 Amputations
🩺 Orthopedic reconstructive surgeries
🩺 Major bone resections
instruments.clinicalTip: 💡 Ensure the bone is positioned securely in the deepest part of the jaws before cutting. This maximizes mechanical leverage and prevents the bone from slipping or shattering outward.

instruments.name: Stille Bone Cutting Forceps
instruments.description: A double-action bone cutting instrument designed to provide increased mechanical advantage for cleanly cutting dense bone.
instruments.detailedDescription: The Stille (or Stille-Liston) bone cutting forceps utilizes a unique double-action hinge mechanism that provides significantly greater biting force with less manual effort compared to single-action cutters. The jaws are exceptionally strong and sharp, designed to sheer through tough bone without crushing the surrounding tissue.
instruments.uses: 🩸 Used for snipping and cutting dense cortical bone, often utilized to enlarge access points, remove bone grafts, or cleanly resect bone margins.
instruments.parts:
🔹 Grip handles
🔹 Double-action hinge joint
🔹 Spring mechanism
🔹 Sharp, opposing cutting blades
instruments.mechanism: ⚙️ The double-action joint works as a compound lever system. It converts a moderate grip force on the handles into a massive, concentrated cutting force at the jaws, making it easier to cut through hard cortical bone.
instruments.commonProcedures:
🩺 Craniotomy
🩺 Spinal decompression (Laminectomy)
🩺 Joint arthroplasty
instruments.clinicalTip: 💡 Because of the powerful compound action, the jaws can easily inadvertently damage adjacent soft tissues. Always ensure surrounding nerves and vessels are completely retracted before engaging the handles.

instruments.name: Ruskin Bone Cutting Forceps
instruments.description: A moderately sized, double-action bone cutter ideal for precision cutting of smaller bones and bone fragments.
instruments.detailedDescription: The Ruskin bone cutting forceps features a curved or straight profile with sharp, narrow blades. Its compound double-action mechanism provides excellent cutting power despite its relatively compact size. This makes it particularly useful for working in confined anatomical spaces where larger, heavier forceps would be too cumbersome or obscure the surgical field.
instruments.uses: 🩸 Used for trimming and cutting small bones, shaping bone grafts, and cleanly removing sharp bony prominences.
instruments.parts:
🔹 Grip handles
🔹 Double-action pivot joint
🔹 Spring-loaded mechanism
🔹 Narrow, sharp cutting jaws
instruments.mechanism: ⚙️ Employs a compound lever system to multiply the cutting force, allowing the surgeon to cleanly shear through small but dense bones with minimal physical exertion.
instruments.commonProcedures:
🩺 Maxillofacial surgery
🩺 Hand and foot surgeries (e.g., Podiatry)
🩺 Small joint orthopedic procedures
instruments.clinicalTip: 💡 Use the curved jaw variation when working around contoured anatomical structures (like the mandible or small joints) to improve your line of sight and approach angle.

instruments.name: Luer Bone Cutting Forceps
instruments.description: A rongeur-style bone cutting instrument with scoop-shaped, sharp-edged jaws used to bite and contour bone.
instruments.detailedDescription: The Luer bone cutting forceps acts as a hybrid between a rongeur and a traditional cutter. It has cup-shaped, sharp-edged jaws that meet precisely to bite off chunks of bone rather than shearing completely across a bone shaft. It typically features a spring-loaded handle for rapid, repetitive action, making it highly effective for contouring irregular bony margins.
instruments.uses: 🩸 Used for nibbling away irregular bone edges, preparing bone surfaces for grafting, or enlarging surgical openings.
instruments.parts:
🔹 Grip handles
🔹 Leaf spring mechanism
🔹 Box joint or double-action joint
🔹 Scoop-shaped / cupped cutting jaws
instruments.mechanism: ⚙️ The sharp, opposing cups gouge out and sheer away small pieces of bone when the handles are compressed, safely capturing the excised bone within the concavity of the jaws.
instruments.commonProcedures:
🩺 Spinal fusion
🩺 Maxillofacial reconstruction
🩺 Orthopedic trauma repair
instruments.clinicalTip: 💡 Clear the cupped jaws with a damp sponge after every few bites. Allowing bone debris to pack into the cups will prevent the jaws from closing completely and can dull the instrument.

instruments.name: Rib Shears
instruments.description: A specialized heavy-duty cutting instrument used exclusively to cut through ribs during thoracic procedures.
instruments.detailedDescription: Rib shears (such as the Bethune, Gluck, or Stille-Giertz patterns) are heavy, highly leveraged instruments featuring a unique blade design. Generally, one blade is hook-shaped or U-shaped to encircle the rib and protect underlying structures, while a sharp guillotine-style or scissor-like upper blade drops down to safely shear the bone.
instruments.uses: 🩸 Severing ribs to gain access to the thoracic cavity or to remove diseased/fractured rib sections.
instruments.parts:
🔹 Extended grip handles
🔹 Heavy-duty pivot joint
🔹 Hooked lower jaw / anvil
🔹 Sliding or pivoting cutting blade
instruments.mechanism: ⚙️ The lower jaw slides completely under the rib to isolate it. The mechanical advantage of the long handles drives the upper blade down through the bone in a powerful, controlled guillotine action.
instruments.commonProcedures:
🩺 Thoracotomy
🩺 Rib resection
🩺 Chest wall reconstruction
instruments.clinicalTip: 💡 Always ensure the hooked lower jaw is kept snug against the underside of the rib. Failing to properly isolate the rib can lead to accidental pinching and severing of the intercostal nerve or vessels running just below the rib margin.

instruments.name: Bone Nibbling Forceps
instruments.description: A broad term for rongeur-style forceps used to gouge out or "nibble" away small pieces of bone and hard tissue.
instruments.detailedDescription: Bone nibbling forceps (often encompassing specific instruments like Jansen or Blumenthal rongeurs) have stout, sharp-edged, spoon-shaped jaws designed for precise, incremental removal of bone. They typically feature a spring-loaded handle that automatically reopens the jaws after each bite, allowing for rapid contouring and debridement of bone edges.
instruments.uses: 🩸 Smoothing rough bone edges, clearing away osteophytes (bone spurs), or removing small bone fragments.
instruments.parts:
🔹 Handles with grip ridges
🔹 Spring-loading mechanism (single or double leaf)
🔹 Hinge joint (single or double action)
🔹 Cupped or spoon-shaped cutting tips
instruments.mechanism: ⚙️ The instrument uses levered mechanical advantage to bite into bone. As the jaws close, the sharp edges sheer off a small fragment, which is captured within the cupped jaws for safe removal from the surgical site.
instruments.commonProcedures:
🩺 Orthopedic debridement
🩺 Exostosis removal
🩺 Dental and maxillofacial surgeries
instruments.clinicalTip: 💡 Never use nibbling forceps to twist or pry bone. The delicate, sharp edges of the cups can easily chip, dull, or break under torsional stress; they are designed strictly for a straightforward biting/cutting action.

instruments.name: Gigli Saw
instruments.description: A flexible wire saw used by surgeons for bone cutting, particularly in amputations and cranial surgeries.
instruments.detailedDescription: The Gigli saw is a flexible, twisted wire cable with sharp cutting edges or barbs along its length. It is designed to cut smoothly through bone with a back-and-forth reciprocating motion. The wire itself is usually disposable and features loops or rings at both ends to attach to separate handles. Its flexible nature allows it to be passed around bones in confined spaces.
instruments.uses: 🩸 Used for cutting through smooth, cylindrical bones or creating cranial flaps where a rigid saw cannot easily reach.
instruments.parts:
🔹 Twisted wire cable
🔹 Cutting barbs/edges
🔹 End loops/rings
instruments.mechanism: ⚙️ The wire is passed around or under the bone, attached to handles, and pulled back and forth in a smooth, continuous friction-cutting motion to sever the bone.
instruments.commonProcedures:
🩺 Craniotomy
🩺 Limb and digit amputations
🩺 Maxillofacial osteotomies
instruments.clinicalTip: 💡 To prevent the wire from binding, kinking, or breaking, keep the handles widely separated to create an obtuse angle (typically around 135 degrees) during the sawing motion.

instruments.name: Gigli Saw Handles
instruments.description: Reusable, detachable handles used to grip and maneuver a Gigli wire saw during bone cutting.
instruments.detailedDescription: Gigli saw handles are typically T-shaped or loop-shaped to provide a secure, ergonomic grip for the surgeon. They feature a hook or snap-clip at the distal end that securely attaches to the loops of the Gigli wire saw. These robust handles allow the surgeon to exert the necessary pulling force for the reciprocating cutting action without the wire slipping or cutting their gloves.
instruments.uses: 🩸 Used exclusively to operate the Gigli wire saw.
instruments.parts:
🔹 T-bar or ring grip
🔹 Rigid shaft
🔹 Distal hook or attachment clasp
instruments.mechanism: ⚙️ The handles act as stable anchors, allowing the user to transmit alternating pulling forces to the attached wire saw, driving the friction-cutting process through the bone.
instruments.commonProcedures:
🩺 Craniotomy
🩺 Limb amputations
instruments.clinicalTip: 💡 Ensure the hooks are fully engaged and locked onto the wire loops before beginning the sawing motion to prevent the wire from flying off under tension and causing injury.

instruments.name: Satterlee Bone Saw
instruments.description: A heavy-duty, manual hand saw featuring a wide blade and a ring handle, used for major bone amputations.
instruments.detailedDescription: The Satterlee bone saw is a traditional, manual surgical saw characterized by a broad, straight, and rigid blade with fine, sharp teeth. It possesses a distinctive ergonomic pistol-grip or D-ring handle, which provides the surgeon with a firm grasp and excellent directional control. Its robust design allows for aggressive manual cutting through thick, large bones.
instruments.uses: 🩸 Manual resection of large, dense long bones when powered equipment is unavailable or contraindicated.
instruments.parts:
🔹 D-ring or pistol-grip handle
🔹 Broad stainless steel blade
🔹 Fine serrated teeth
instruments.mechanism: ⚙️ Operated manually with a push-pull (reciprocating) stroke, using the sharp teeth to carve a straight kerf (groove) through the bone.
instruments.commonProcedures:
🩺 Major limb amputations (e.g., above-knee or below-knee amputations)
🩺 Large bone resections
instruments.clinicalTip: 💡 Always start the cut by drawing the blade backward gently to create a preliminary groove. This prevents the blade from skipping off the curved bone surface and damaging adjacent soft tissue.

instruments.name: Amputation Saw
instruments.description: A classic, robust manual saw used for cleanly severing the shafts of large bones during limb removal.
instruments.detailedDescription: An amputation saw (such as the Charriere or Langenbeck patterns) is designed specifically to cleanly sever large bones without splintering. It features a sturdy, straight blade, often with cross-cut teeth designed to clear bone dust from the cutting path to prevent jamming. The handle is aligned to allow the surgeon to apply smooth, downward pressure efficiently.
instruments.uses: 🩸 Severing the bone shaft evenly during limb removal procedures.
instruments.parts:
🔹 Heavy-duty grip handle
🔹 Long, rigid straight blade
🔹 Cross-cut or fine teeth
instruments.mechanism: ⚙️ The surgeon uses smooth, long, and steady back-and-forth strokes, allowing the weight of the saw and the sharpness of the teeth to cut the bone rather than applying excessive downward force.
instruments.commonProcedures:
🩺 Limb amputations
🩺 Forensic pathology / Autopsy
instruments.clinicalTip: 💡 Keep the saw blade perfectly perpendicular to the long axis of the bone. Ensuring a flat, even bone stump is crucial for patient comfort and subsequent prosthetic fitting.

instruments.name: Oscillating Bone Saw
instruments.description: A high-speed, powered surgical saw whose blade vibrates side-to-side in a narrow arc to cut bone.
instruments.detailedDescription: The oscillating bone saw is a standard powered orthopedic instrument operated via battery or pneumatics. Its blade oscillates (swings left and right) at high speeds over a very small angle. This specialized motion cuts rigid structures like bone efficiently but tends to harmlessly push or bounce off compliant soft tissues, greatly reducing the risk of accidental neurovascular injury.
instruments.uses: 🩸 Making precise, straight cuts (osteotomies) in bones, especially in confined spaces or near delicate soft tissues.
instruments.parts:
🔹 Powered handpiece (battery/pneumatic)
🔹 Trigger mechanism
🔹 Locking collet / chuck
🔹 Disposable oscillating blade
instruments.mechanism: ⚙️ High-speed micro-oscillation of the blade cuts hard cortical bone through rapid friction and micro-fracture without wrapping or tearing elastic soft tissues.
instruments.commonProcedures:
🩺 Total joint arthroplasty (knee, hip)
🩺 Osteotomies
🩺 Cast removal
instruments.clinicalTip: 💡 Continuously irrigate the cutting site with cool sterile saline. The high-speed oscillation generates significant friction and heat, which can cause thermal necrosis (death) of the surrounding bone if not actively cooled.

instruments.name: Reciprocating Bone Saw
instruments.description: A powered surgical saw whose blade moves rapidly back and forth in a linear, piston-like motion.
instruments.detailedDescription: Unlike the oscillating saw, the reciprocating saw drives a straight blade in a rapid in-and-out (push-pull) motion. Powered pneumatically or via battery, the blade is typically narrow and pointed. This design allows it to be plunged directly into bone or used to cut deep, narrow channels where a wider oscillating blade would not fit.
instruments.uses: 🩸 Making deep, plunging cuts, or cutting bone in deep, narrow anatomical cavities.
instruments.parts:
🔹 Powered handpiece
🔹 Trigger mechanism
🔹 Blade chuck
🔹 Narrow, straight reciprocating blade
instruments.mechanism: ⚙️ Uses high-speed linear, push-pull motion to drive the serrated blade through the bone, cutting similarly to a traditional hand saw but at extreme speeds.
instruments.commonProcedures:
🩺 Sternotomy (splitting the sternum for open-heart surgery)
🩺 Total joint arthroplasty
🩺 Spinal surgeries
instruments.clinicalTip: 💡 Because the blade aggressively moves in and out, be acutely aware of what lies *behind* or *deep* to the bone being cut to prevent the tip from plunging into and damaging vital underlying structures (like the heart or spinal cord).

instruments.name: Straight Bone Chisel
instruments.description: A straight, flat orthopedic instrument with a beveled cutting edge used to cut, shave, or shape bone.
instruments.detailedDescription: The straight bone chisel features a flat, rigid blade that terminates in a sharp cutting edge. The handle is solid, often made of heavy stainless steel or synthetic shock-absorbing materials (like Tufnol), designed to withstand repeated strikes from a surgical mallet. The straight design allows the surgeon to make precise, linear cuts into cortical or cancellous bone.
instruments.uses: 🩸 Used for cutting, splitting, or reshaping bone surfaces.
instruments.parts:
🔹 Solid striking handle
🔹 Straight metal shaft
🔹 Sharp beveled cutting edge
instruments.mechanism: ⚙️ The surgeon places the cutting edge against the target bone and strikes the proximal handle with a surgical mallet. The kinetic force is transmitted straight down the rigid shaft, driving the sharp edge into the bone to shear or cut it.
instruments.commonProcedures:
🩺 Osteotomy
🩺 Bone graft harvesting
🩺 Orthopedic fracture repair
instruments.clinicalTip: 💡 When using a mallet, focus your eyes on the cutting edge of the chisel against the bone, not on the handle where you are striking. This ensures precision in the cut.

instruments.name: Curved Bone Chisel
instruments.description: A bone-cutting instrument with a gently curved blade used to contour or scoop bone in curved anatomical areas.
instruments.detailedDescription: The curved bone chisel has a shaft or tip that curves slightly, allowing it to adapt to the natural convexities or concavities of bones. Unlike a straight chisel, the curve helps the surgeon skim along the surface of a bone or create shallow, sweeping cuts without plunging too deeply into the medullary canal. It is highly beneficial when a straight approach would be obstructed by surrounding soft tissues.
instruments.uses: 🩸 Used for contouring bone surfaces, removing rounded osteophytes, or accessing bone margins at difficult angles.
instruments.parts:
🔹 Striking handle
🔹 Curved shank or blade
🔹 Sharp beveled cutting edge
instruments.mechanism: ⚙️ Driven by a mallet, the curved blade follows an arced path through the bone, lifting or shaving the bone in a controlled, contoured manner rather than a straight line.
instruments.commonProcedures:
🩺 Joint arthroplasty
🩺 Osteophyte (bone spur) excision
🩺 Spinal decompression
instruments.clinicalTip: 💡 The curve of the chisel should be intentionally matched to the anatomical curve of the bone being worked on to prevent inadvertent splintering.

instruments.name: Lambotte Chisel
instruments.description: A flat, widely used orthopedic chisel forged from a single piece of stainless steel for precise bone cutting.
instruments.detailedDescription: The Lambotte chisel is a classic orthopedic instrument recognized by its straight, flat profile and completely metallic construction. The handle and the blade are continuous, forged from a single piece of stainless steel. The blade tapers down to a very fine, sharp cutting edge. It is available in various millimeter widths to accommodate different sizes of bone and surgical needs.
instruments.uses: 🩸 Used for the precise sectioning of bone, creating osteotomies, and harvesting block bone grafts.
instruments.parts:
🔹 Continuous stainless steel flat handle
🔹 Straight tapered blade
🔹 Fine single-beveled cutting edge
instruments.mechanism: ⚙️ The solid one-piece steel construction provides excellent tactile feedback to the surgeon and ensures highly efficient energy transfer from the mallet to the cutting edge, allowing for clean, straight cuts.
instruments.commonProcedures:
🩺 Iliac crest bone graft harvesting
🩺 Corrective osteotomies
🩺 General orthopedic trauma surgery
instruments.clinicalTip: 💡 Because the entire instrument is smooth metal, striking it with a metal mallet can cause the mallet to slip. A mallet with a nylon or polymeric face is often preferred to provide a safer, "dead-blow" strike.

instruments.name: Stille Chisel
instruments.description: A heavy-duty orthopedic chisel with a thick blade and robust handle designed to withstand forceful mallet strikes.
instruments.detailedDescription: The Stille chisel is engineered specifically for heavy bone work. It features a thick, robust blade that resists bending or breaking under immense stress. The handle is often thicker or bulbous, providing a substantial striking surface that absorbs and transmits the heavy kinetic force of mallet blows efficiently through dense bone.
instruments.uses: 🩸 Used for cutting through thick, dense cortical bone or executing major bone resections.
instruments.parts:
🔹 Heavy-duty striking handle
🔹 Thick, rigid shaft
🔹 Wide, sharp beveled cutting edge
instruments.mechanism: ⚙️ The heavy-weight construction allows the instrument to withstand significant impact, driving the sharp edge through the hardest, thickest cortical bone cleanly without instrument deformation.
instruments.commonProcedures:
🩺 Major joint arthroplasty (Hip, Knee)
🩺 Large bone osteotomies
🩺 Amputations
instruments.clinicalTip: 💡 Always maintain a very firm grip on the handle. The heavy mallet strikes required to drive this thick chisel through cortical bone can cause significant recoil.

instruments.name: Gouge Chisel
instruments.description: A chisel with a curved, trough-like blade used to scoop out channels or harvest soft cancellous bone.
instruments.detailedDescription: A gouge is a specialized type of chisel featuring a concave, semi-circular, or U-shaped blade. The cutting edge is curved, which allows the instrument to literally scoop into the bone and remove trough-shaped segments. It is highly effective for hollowing out bone cavities, decorticating surfaces, or retrieving spongy cancellous bone for grafting purposes.
instruments.uses: 🩸 Used for harvesting cancellous bone, creating troughs or channels in bone, and decorticating bone surfaces.
instruments.parts:
🔹 Striking handle
🔹 U-shaped or half-pipe blade shaft
🔹 Curved (concave) cutting edge
instruments.mechanism: ⚙️ When struck with a mallet, the curved edge cuts a circular or semi-circular path into the tissue, creating a groove and lifting a "scoop" of bone inside the trough of the blade.
instruments.commonProcedures:
🩺 Bone grafting
🩺 Spinal fusion
🩺 Preparing bone beds for implants
instruments.clinicalTip: 💡 Use a gentle prying motion after striking to lift the scooped bone graft out of the donor site, being careful not to use the delicate curved edge of the gouge as a heavy lever, which could chip it.

instruments.name: Single-Bevel Bone Chisel
instruments.description: A chisel with an angled slope on only one side of the cutting edge, used to shave or cut bone perfectly flat.
instruments.detailedDescription: The single-bevel bone chisel is defined by its cutting edge geometry: it features a completely flat surface on one side and a sloped (beveled) surface on the other. This specific design forces the chisel to cut straight along the flat side while wedging and pushing the excised bone fragment up and away on the beveled side.
instruments.uses: 🩸 Used for shaving bone flat, removing exostoses (bone spurs), and performing precise contouring osteotomies.
instruments.parts:
🔹 Striking handle
🔹 Blade shaft
🔹 Single-bevel cutting edge (one side flat, one side angled)
instruments.mechanism: ⚙️ The flat side slides smoothly against the remaining host bone, dictating the straight path of the cut, while the beveled side lifts and separates the waste bone fragment.
instruments.commonProcedures:
🩺 Bunionectomy
🩺 Excision of osteophytes
🩺 General orthopedic contouring
instruments.clinicalTip: 💡 Remember the cardinal rule of the single-bevel chisel: "Flat side to the host bone, bevel side to the waste bone." This ensures the remaining bone surface is left perfectly flat and smooth.

instruments.name: Double-Bevel Bone Chisel
instruments.description: A bone cutting instrument (technically called an osteotome) with slopes on both sides of the cutting edge, used to split bone evenly.
instruments.detailedDescription: While often grouped with chisels, an instrument with a double-bevel is technically termed an "osteotome." It has a cutting edge that tapers evenly on both sides, resembling a wedge. Unlike a single-bevel chisel that shaves bone flat, the double-bevel design is meant to split or cut bone evenly down the middle without driving the blade in one specific lateral direction.
instruments.uses: 🩸 Used for splitting bone, creating precise divisions of a bone shaft, and cutting bone grafts.
instruments.parts:
🔹 Striking handle
🔹 Blade shaft
🔹 Double-bevel (wedge-shaped) cutting edge
instruments.mechanism: ⚙️ When driven into bone, the symmetrical wedge shape forces the bone to part equally on both sides of the blade, tracking in a straight line and splitting the tissue.
instruments.commonProcedures:
🩺 Corrective osteotomies
🩺 Splitting the sternum (sternotomy)
🩺 Dividing bone grafts
instruments.clinicalTip: 💡 Because the double-bevel acts as a true wedge, driving it deeply into brittle or sclerotic bone can cause the bone to propagate a fracture line in unintended directions. Ensure the bone is properly supported during use.

instruments.name: Cobb Curette
instruments.description: A heavy-duty orthopedic curette with a broad, textured handle used for aggressively scraping bone and tough fibrous tissue.
instruments.detailedDescription: The Cobb curette is designed to withstand significant manual force. It features a thick, stout handle—often knurled or octagonal—that provides a non-slip grip. The shaft is thick and rigid, terminating in a sharp, scoop or cup-shaped tip. It is widely utilized in major orthopedic and spinal surgeries to strip heavy periosteum from bone or to hollow out bone cavities.
instruments.uses: 🩸 Scraping tough periosteum, decorticating bone surfaces, and removing dense pathological tissue.
instruments.parts:
🔹 Heavy knurled or octagonal handle
🔹 Thick, rigid shaft
🔹 Sharp scoop or cup-shaped tip
instruments.mechanism: ⚙️ The sharp edges of the scoop scrape and gouge dense tissue or bone when the surgeon applies firm pressure and pulls or pushes the instrument against the anatomical structure.
instruments.commonProcedures:
🩺 Spinal fusion
🩺 Laminectomy
🩺 Major joint reconstruction
instruments.clinicalTip: 💡 The heavy handle provides excellent leverage; however, the surgeon must carefully control their force to avoid unexpectedly plunging the tip through the bone cortex or into delicate underlying structures like the spinal cord.

instruments.name: Brun Curette
instruments.description: A bone curette with a long, slender handle and a cup-shaped tip used for scraping and debriding soft bone or tissue.
instruments.detailedDescription: The Brun (or Bruns) curette features a long, somewhat slender profile compared to the Cobb. It has a hexagonal or smooth handle and a straight shaft ending in a small, oval or round cup with sharp inner edges. Its longer reach makes it ideal for reaching deep into bone cavities or narrow surgical fields to scoop out soft cancellous bone, cysts, or infected material.
instruments.uses: 🩸 Debridement of bone cysts, scooping out cancellous bone for grafting, and cleaning infected bone sites.
instruments.parts:
🔹 Hexagonal or smooth handle
🔹 Long, slender shaft
🔹 Sharp oval or round cup tip
instruments.mechanism: ⚙️ The sharp edges of the cup scrape away soft tissue or bone while the concave center safely captures the debris for easy removal from the surgical site.
instruments.commonProcedures:
🩺 Bone grafting
🩺 Tumor or cyst excision
🩺 Orthopedic debridement (e.g., osteomyelitis treatment)
instruments.clinicalTip: 💡 Use a controlled pulling motion to safely scrape tissue toward yourself. Pushing blindly into a cavity increases the risk of perforating the bone or pushing pathological debris deeper into the defect.

instruments.name: Volkmann Curette
instruments.description: A spoon-shaped curette, often double-ended, used for scraping bone and debriding soft tissue.
instruments.detailedDescription: The Volkmann curette is a highly versatile instrument that features an oval, spoon-like tip with sharp cutting edges. It is available in various sizes and is frequently manufactured as a double-ended instrument with a different sized scoop on each end. It is widely used across orthopedics and general surgery for scraping away necrotic tissue, bone fragments, or soft tissue attachments.
instruments.uses: 🩸 Scraping bone surfaces, removing granulomatous tissue, and debriding open wounds.
instruments.parts:
🔹 Flat or bulbous central handle (if double-ended)
🔹 Straight or slightly angled shaft
🔹 Sharp spoon-shaped tip(s)
instruments.mechanism: ⚙️ The sharp edges shave or scoop out tissue when manual pressure is applied and the instrument is dragged firmly across the tissue surface.
instruments.commonProcedures:
🩺 Orthopedic wound debridement
🩺 Joint arthroplasty preparation
🩺 Soft tissue mass excisions
instruments.clinicalTip: 💡 When utilizing a double-ended Volkmann curette, always maintain spatial awareness of the proximal sharp tip near your hand to avoid tearing your surgical gloves or snagging the sterile drapes.

instruments.name: Lucas Curette
instruments.description: A spoon-shaped curette with angled shanks primarily used in small bone surgeries and maxillofacial procedures for delicate debridement.
instruments.detailedDescription: The Lucas curette features a spoon-shaped blade attached to an angled shank, specifically designed to navigate into small sockets or cavities. It is typically a double-ended instrument with mirror-image working ends to allow access from multiple angles. It is excellent for removing debris, small cysts, or granulation tissue from confined osseous defects.
instruments.uses: 🩸 Debridement of small bone defects, removal of cysts, and clearing granulation tissue.
instruments.parts:
🔹 Cylindrical grip handle
🔹 Angled shanks
🔹 Double-ended, spoon-shaped working tips
instruments.mechanism: ⚙️ The angled tip is inserted into a small cavity and rotated or pulled along the walls to cleanly scrape out pathological tissue without damaging the surrounding healthy bone.
instruments.commonProcedures:
🩺 Maxillofacial trauma repair
🩺 Dental extractions and alveolar bone grafting
🩺 Minor hand and foot bone procedures
instruments.clinicalTip: 💡 The angled shank provides excellent leverage and visibility. Use the convex back of the spoon to gently palpate the floor of the cavity before using the sharp concave edge to scrape.

instruments.name: Dermal Curette
instruments.description: A small, fine instrument with a sharp-edged loop or ring used to scrape and shave superficial skin lesions.
instruments.detailedDescription: The dermal curette (such as a Fox curette) features a small, sharp loop, ring, or shallow cup at the end of a pen-like handle. It is designed to shave off epidermal and superficial dermal lesions while preserving underlying healthy tissue. In orthopedics, it is often utilized during the management of cutaneous lesions around surgical sites or for very superficial wound debridement.
instruments.uses: 🩸 Scraping superficial necrotic tissue, warts, or benign skin lesions near the surgical field.
instruments.parts:
🔹 Pen-like handle
🔹 Short, fine shaft
🔹 Sharp ring or semi-sharp shallow scoop tip
instruments.mechanism: ⚙️ The sharp loop is drawn across the skin or tissue surface to cleanly slice and scrape away superficial lesions with precise, shallow strokes.
instruments.commonProcedures:
🩺 Superficial wound debridement
🩺 Dermatological excisions
🩺 Paronychia or nail bed procedures
instruments.clinicalTip: 💡 Stretch the surrounding skin taut with your non-dominant hand to provide a firm, flat surface. This allows the curette to smoothly shave the lesion without snagging or bunching the skin.

instruments.name: Bone Curette
instruments.description: A general classification for a scoop-like surgical instrument used to harvest bone graft or clear pathological tissue from bone.
instruments.detailedDescription: A general bone curette (which may encompass patterns like the Simon or Hardy) consists of a sturdy handle and a sharp, cup-shaped tip. It is fundamentally used to scrape away soft cancellous bone, marrow, or diseased tissue from the medullary canal or cortical bone surfaces. The depth and width of the cup can vary greatly depending on the required application.
instruments.uses: 🩸 Bone graft harvesting, marrow cavity preparation, and general bone debridement.
instruments.parts:
🔹 Solid handle (often ribbed for grip)
🔹 Rigid central shaft
🔹 Cupped, sharp cutting tip
instruments.mechanism: ⚙️ The instrument uses a gouging or scraping action to cut bone fragments or soft tissue, collecting the excised material within the concavity of the cup.
instruments.commonProcedures:
🩺 Open reduction internal fixation (ORIF)
🩺 Bone grafting
🩺 Intramedullary nailing preparation
instruments.clinicalTip: 💡 Select a curette size that closely matches the diameter of the bone cavity. Using a curette that is too large will wedge in the cavity and risk fracturing the surrounding cortical bone, while one that is too small will be inefficient.

instruments.name: Micro Ring Curette
instruments.description: A highly delicate, fine-tipped ring curette used in microsurgery and small joint procedures for meticulous tissue removal.
instruments.detailedDescription: The micro ring curette features an extremely small, circular, sharp-edged ring at the tip of a very fine shaft. It is designed for precise, microscopic scraping in confined anatomical spaces. It allows surgeons to remove minute soft tissue attachments from small joints or perform delicate bony decompressions around nerves under microscopic magnification.
instruments.uses: 🩸 Removing microscopic tissue tags, cleaning small articular surfaces, and dissecting safely around delicate neurovascular structures.
instruments.parts:
🔹 Fine, knurled pen-grip handle
🔹 Very thin, delicate shaft
🔹 Microscopic sharp ring tip
instruments.mechanism: ⚙️ The tiny sharp ring gently scrapes away microscopic amounts of tissue, offering high precision and visibility through the center of the ring.
instruments.commonProcedures:
🩺 Hand and wrist surgery (e.g., carpal bone procedures)
🩺 Spinal microdiscectomy
🩺 Peripheral nerve surgery
instruments.clinicalTip: 💡 Because the shaft and tip are highly delicate, you must never use this instrument as a lever or apply heavy scraping force. It is designed for finesse, and heavy force will easily bend the shaft or snap the tip.

instruments.name: Straight Osteotome
instruments.description: A flat, straight orthopedic instrument with a double-beveled cutting edge used for cutting and splitting bone.
instruments.detailedDescription: The straight osteotome features a solid handle, a straight shaft, and a symmetrical, double-beveled tip. Unlike a chisel, which is single-beveled, the osteotome tapers equally on both sides to form a wedge. This symmetrical design allows it to cut straight into the bone and split it cleanly without deflecting to one side. It is a fundamental tool for making precise, linear bone cuts.
instruments.uses: 🩸 Splitting, cutting, or marking bone, and preparing bone graft sites.
instruments.parts:
🔹 Solid striking handle
🔹 Straight rigid shaft
🔹 Symmetrical, double-beveled cutting edge
instruments.mechanism: ⚙️ Driven by a surgical mallet, the symmetrical wedge design cuts straight into bone, displacing the osseous tissue equally on both sides to create a precise, linear split or cut.
instruments.commonProcedures:
🩺 Corrective osteotomies
🩺 Bone graft harvesting
🩺 Orthopedic fracture reduction
instruments.clinicalTip: 💡 Ensure the blade is aligned exactly with the intended plane of the cut before striking, as the double bevel will track straight down the path of least resistance.

instruments.name: Curved Osteotome
instruments.description: An osteotome with a gently curved shaft or blade used to contour bone or access anatomically rounded areas.
instruments.detailedDescription: The curved osteotome has a double-beveled cutting edge on a shaft that sweeps into a distinct curve. This curvature allows surgeons to navigate around cylindrical bones or perform osteotomies in tight, curved anatomical spaces where a straight instrument would be obstructed. It is highly effective for creating contoured cuts or lifting bone flaps.
instruments.uses: 🩸 Contouring bone, creating curved osteotomies, and accessing hard-to-reach osseous structures.
instruments.parts:
🔹 Solid striking handle
🔹 Curved shaft or blade
🔹 Symmetrical, double-beveled cutting edge
instruments.mechanism: ⚙️ Transmits the mallet's kinetic force along a curved path, allowing the double-beveled edge to split or cut bone along convex or concave anatomical surfaces safely.
instruments.commonProcedures:
🩺 Major joint arthroplasty (e.g., hip or knee)
🩺 Spinal surgery
🩺 Pelvic osteotomies
instruments.clinicalTip: 💡 Match the curvature of the osteotome to the natural curve of the bone being cut to prevent unintended breaching of the far cortex or splintering of the bone shaft.

instruments.name: Lambotte Osteotome
instruments.description: A classic, flat osteotome forged from a single piece of stainless steel, characterized by its straight profile and double-beveled edge.
instruments.detailedDescription: The Lambotte osteotome is a widely used orthopedic instrument made of continuous, solid stainless steel from handle to tip. It features a flat, straight blade that tapers down to a sharp double-beveled edge. Its one-piece design provides excellent tactile feedback to the surgeon and ensures highly efficient energy transfer from the mallet to the bone.
instruments.uses: 🩸 Precise bone cutting, sectioning cortical bone, and harvesting bone grafts.
instruments.parts:
🔹 Continuous stainless steel handle
🔹 Flat, straight blade
🔹 Sharp double-beveled cutting edge
instruments.mechanism: ⚙️ The solid steel construction efficiently transfers the kinetic energy of the mallet strike directly to the wedge-shaped tip, splitting bone symmetrically without absorbing the force in the handle.
instruments.commonProcedures:
🩺 Iliac crest bone grafting
🩺 Corrective osteotomies
🩺 General orthopedic trauma repair
instruments.clinicalTip: 💡 Because the handle is smooth, solid steel, use a mallet with a synthetic or polymer face (a "dead-blow" mallet) to prevent the mallet from slipping or glancing off the handle during strikes.

instruments.name: Stille Osteotome
instruments.description: A heavy-duty osteotome with a thick, robust blade designed for cutting through dense cortical bone.
instruments.detailedDescription: The Stille osteotome is engineered for major bone work where significant force is required. It features a thick, rigid blade and a substantial handle that resists bending or breaking under the stress of heavy mallet strikes. The broad, double-beveled tip allows for straight, forceful splitting of the hardest, thickest bones in the body.
instruments.uses: 🩸 Splitting or cutting thick, dense cortical bone and performing major bone resections.
instruments.parts:
🔹 Heavy-duty striking handle
🔹 Thick, rigid shaft
🔹 Wide double-beveled cutting edge
instruments.mechanism: ⚙️ Its heavy weight and rigid construction absorb and transmit high-impact mallet strikes, driving the symmetrical wedge through dense bone without instrument deformation or chatter.
instruments.commonProcedures:
🩺 Major joint arthroplasty
🩺 Large bone osteotomies
🩺 Amputations
instruments.clinicalTip: 💡 Maintain a very firm, controlled grip with your non-dominant hand. The heavy mallet strikes required to drive this instrument through dense cortical bone can cause significant recoil.

instruments.name: Hibbs Osteotome
instruments.description: A specialized osteotome featuring a hexagonal handle and a sturdy blade, often used in spinal and heavy orthopedic surgeries.
instruments.detailedDescription: The Hibbs osteotome is distinguished by its heavy, hexagonal or solidly rounded handle that provides a highly secure, non-slip grip. The blade is straight or gently curved, tapering to a sharp double-beveled edge. It is specifically designed to provide high precision, stability, and durability when working on dense bones in anatomically sensitive areas, such as the spine.
instruments.uses: 🩸 Precise bone cutting, harvesting bone grafts, and executing osteotomies in dense bone.
instruments.parts:
🔹 Hexagonal or heavy solid handle
🔹 Sturdy, rigid blade
🔹 Double-beveled cutting edge
instruments.mechanism: ⚙️ The ergonomic, angled handle provides superior grip and rotational control, directing the mallet's force precisely through the double-bevel to split bone cleanly while minimizing accidental slippage.
instruments.commonProcedures:
🩺 Spinal fusion
🩺 Laminectomy
🩺 Pelvic or heavy bone reconstructive surgery
instruments.clinicalTip: 💡 The hexagonal handle is excellent for preventing the instrument from rotating in your hand. Use this feature to maintain strict alignment when precise, unyielding cuts are critical, such as during spinal osteotomies.

instruments.name: Kerrison Rongeur
instruments.description: A specialized bone-biting instrument with a punch-action tip used to remove small bites of bone, particularly in the spine.
instruments.detailedDescription: The Kerrison rongeur is a highly specialized instrument extensively used in neurosurgery and orthopedics. It features a long, slender shaft and a distal cutting foot (or shoe) that punches upwards or downwards. The bite size typically ranges from 1 mm to 5 mm, allowing for meticulous, millimeter-by-millimeter removal of bone to relieve pressure on neural structures.
instruments.uses: 🩸 Removing bone to decompress nerves or access the spinal canal.
instruments.parts:
🔹 Grip handle
🔹 Spring mechanism
🔹 Long slender shaft
🔹 Distal cutting footplate/shoe (up-biting or down-biting)
instruments.mechanism: ⚙️ Operates with a sliding punch-action mechanism. When the handle is squeezed, the inner shaft slides forward to close against the stationary footplate, cleanly shearing off a small piece of bone.
instruments.commonProcedures:
🩺 Laminectomy
🩺 Spinal decompression
🩺 Microdiscectomy
instruments.clinicalTip: 💡 Always verify the bite size and cutting direction (up or down) before handing it to the surgeon, as the wrong size or angle can risk damaging the spinal cord.

instruments.name: Ferris-Smith Kerrison Rongeur
instruments.description: A heavy-duty version of the Kerrison rongeur designed for removing thicker, denser bone.
instruments.detailedDescription: The Ferris-Smith Kerrison rongeur incorporates a wider, more robust handle and shaft compared to the standard Kerrison. It is built to withstand greater mechanical force, making it ideal for biting through dense cortical bone or hypertrophied ligaments, often encountered in the cervical or lumbar spine.
instruments.uses: 🩸 Removing tough, dense bone or calcified osteophytes in confined spaces.
instruments.parts:
🔹 Heavy-duty grip handle
🔹 Reinforced shaft
🔹 Distal cutting footplate
🔹 Spring mechanism
instruments.mechanism: ⚙️ Uses a sliding punch-action mechanism similar to a standard Kerrison but is reinforced to deliver higher shear force without the shaft bending or the instrument deforming.
instruments.commonProcedures:
🩺 Lumbar laminectomy
🩺 Foraminotomy
🩺 Spinal fusion preparation
instruments.clinicalTip: 💡 Keep the sliding track meticulously clean of bone debris during the procedure. The heavier bites taken by this instrument can quickly pack bone into the track, jamming the sliding mechanism.

instruments.name: Pituitary Rongeur
instruments.description: A slender, grasping rongeur used to remove soft tissue, herniated disc material, or tumors from confined spaces.
instruments.detailedDescription: Also known as a pituitary forceps, this instrument features a pistol-grip handle and a long, narrow shaft ending in small, cupped jaws. Unlike bone-biting rongeurs, the jaws are typically fenestrated (windowed) and designed with blunt or semi-sharp edges. They are meant to grasp, hold, and pull soft tissue rather than sheer through solid bone.
instruments.uses: 🩸 Extracting soft tissue, herniated disc fragments, or tumors.
instruments.parts:
🔹 Pistol-grip handle
🔹 Long slender shaft
🔹 Cupped, often fenestrated jaws
instruments.mechanism: ⚙️ Squeezing the handle closes the cupped jaws to firmly grasp soft tissue, which is then extracted with a pulling motion out of the surgical cavity.
instruments.commonProcedures:
🩺 Discectomy
🩺 Transsphenoidal hypophysectomy
🩺 Removal of loose bodies in joints
instruments.clinicalTip: 💡 Do not use this instrument to bite or pry bone. The delicate jaws and slender shaft will easily bend or break under the stress of hard bone.

instruments.name: Leksell Rongeur
instruments.description: A heavy, double-action bone-biting rongeur with slightly curved or straight scoop-shaped jaws.
instruments.detailedDescription: The Leksell rongeur is a workhorse in major orthopedic and spinal procedures, providing exceptional cutting power due to its double-action joint. The jaws are trough-like or scoop-shaped, allowing the surgeon to gouge out substantial pieces of bone efficiently and capture the debris within the cups for safe removal.
instruments.uses: 🩸 Removing large bites of bone, such as spinous processes, or expanding bony openings.
instruments.parts:
🔹 Plier-style handles
🔹 Double-action hinge
🔹 Leaf spring
🔹 Scoop-shaped, sharp jaws
instruments.mechanism: ⚙️ The double-action compound lever multiplies the force from the user's grip, allowing the sharp, cupped jaws to cleanly bite through dense bone with minimal manual effort.
instruments.commonProcedures:
🩺 Laminectomy
🩺 Thoracic surgery
🩺 General orthopedic debridement
instruments.clinicalTip: 💡 Because of the powerful double-action mechanism, it requires excellent visibility and careful soft tissue retraction to avoid accidentally biting into adjacent nerves or blood vessels.

instruments.name: Stille-Luer Rongeur
instruments.description: A large, double-action rongeur with wide, deep cup-shaped jaws designed for heavy bone removal and contouring.
instruments.detailedDescription: The Stille-Luer rongeur is characterized by its large size and wide, deep cups. It utilizes a robust compound double-action mechanism to sheer off large chunks of bone. The jaws can be straight or angled, making it a highly versatile instrument for aggressively contouring irregular bone surfaces or resecting large segments.
instruments.uses: 🩸 Contouring bone surfaces, resecting rib segments, or clearing large osteophytes.
instruments.parts:
🔹 Sturdy grip handles
🔹 Double-action pivot joints
🔹 Leaf spring mechanism
🔹 Large, deep cupped cutting jaws
instruments.mechanism: ⚙️ Compound leverage translates moderate grip pressure into immense biting force at the jaws, capturing the large excised bone fragments within the deep cups.
instruments.commonProcedures:
🩺 Thoracotomy
🩺 Pelvic surgery
🩺 Major joint arthroplasty
instruments.clinicalTip: 💡 Regularly clear the deep cups of bone fragments with a moist sponge to maintain cutting efficiency and prevent the jaws from jamming open.

instruments.name: Blumenthal Rongeur
instruments.description: A delicate, angled rongeur used for fine bone nibbling and contouring in tight anatomical spaces.
instruments.detailedDescription: The Blumenthal rongeur features slightly angled, spoon-shaped tips and is typically manufactured with a single or double-action hinge. It is highly favored in maxillofacial, dental, and small bone surgeries where high precision is more critical than heavy cutting power. The angle of the jaws improves the surgeon's line of sight into small cavities.
instruments.uses: 🩸 Smoothing rough bone edges and removing small bone fragments or spicules.
instruments.parts:
🔹 Grip handles
🔹 Spring mechanism
🔹 Angled, spoon-shaped cutting jaws
instruments.mechanism: ⚙️ The jaws close firmly to nibble away small amounts of bone. The angled design allows the user to bite and contour bone while maintaining a clear view over the top of the instrument.
instruments.commonProcedures:
🩺 Maxillofacial trauma repair
🩺 Dental extractions and alveolar contouring
🩺 Podiatry and hand surgery
instruments.clinicalTip: 💡 The angled tip is perfect for reaching into sockets or contoured areas. Use it to gently "nibble" and smooth the bone rather than attempting to take massive, forceful bites.

instruments.name: Single-Action Rongeur
instruments.description: A basic bone-biting instrument with a single pivot point, offering direct force transfer without compound leverage.
instruments.detailedDescription: A single-action rongeur operates on a simple hinge, much like a pair of scissors or standard pliers. It relies entirely on the surgeon's grip strength without the assistance of compound leverage. It is typically smaller and used for cutting softer bone, cartilage, or for pediatric patients where massive force is not required.
instruments.uses: 🩸 Removing small pieces of relatively soft or thin bone and cartilage.
instruments.parts:
🔹 Grip handles
🔹 Single pivot joint (box lock or screw)
🔹 Cupped jaws
🔹 Leaf spring
instruments.mechanism: ⚙️ Squeezing the handles closes the jaws directly around the single pivot point, cutting the bone through direct 1:1 manual force.
instruments.commonProcedures:
🩺 Minor orthopedic procedures
🩺 ENT surgeries (e.g., sinus procedures)
🩺 Pediatric orthopedics
instruments.clinicalTip: 💡 Because it lacks a compound action, attempting to cut dense cortical bone with a single-action rongeur can quickly lead to surgeon hand fatigue or damage to the instrument's hinge.

instruments.name: Double-Action Rongeur
instruments.description: A heavy-duty bone rongeur featuring two pivot points to multiply cutting force, minimizing manual effort.
instruments.detailedDescription: The double-action rongeur is a broad classification for instruments that incorporate a compound hinge mechanism (such as the Leksell or Stille patterns). This design absorbs the immense strain of cutting dense bone, requiring significantly less manual effort from the surgeon while delivering a powerful, clean bite that resists splintering the bone.
instruments.uses: 🩸 Resecting thick, dense cortical bone efficiently and quickly.
instruments.parts:
🔹 Grip handles
🔹 Two pivot joints (compound hinge)
🔹 Cupped cutting jaws
🔹 Spring mechanism
instruments.mechanism: ⚙️ The dual hinges work as a compound lever system, multiplying the input force applied to the handles to produce a vastly stronger cutting force at the jaws.
instruments.commonProcedures:
🩺 Laminectomy
🩺 Craniotomy
🩺 Major orthopedic bone resections
instruments.clinicalTip: 💡 The powerful mechanical advantage means you lose some tactile feedback compared to a single-action tool. Proceed cautiously to avoid biting deeper into the bone than intended.

instruments.name: Mayo Scissors
instruments.description: Heavy-duty, versatile surgical scissors used for cutting tough tissue, fascia, or heavy sutures.
instruments.detailedDescription: Mayo scissors are characterized by their stout, strong blades and a relatively balanced handle-to-blade ratio. They are available in straight and curved varieties. The blades are thick and typically have blunt-blunt or sharp-blunt tips, allowing them to withstand significant force without bending or losing alignment.
instruments.uses: 🩸 Cutting heavy connective tissue, fascia, muscle, or thick suture material.
instruments.parts:
🔹 Ring handles
🔹 Box lock joint
🔹 Stout, heavy blades
🔹 Blunt or sharp tips
instruments.mechanism: ⚙️ Operates using a standard shearing action, with heavy blades designed to shear through dense structures without the blades splaying apart.
instruments.commonProcedures:
🩺 Orthopedic fascial closure
🩺 General surgery exposures
🩺 Joint capsule dissection
instruments.clinicalTip: 💡 Never use your curved Mayo scissors to cut sutures. Suture material will rapidly dull the cutting edge, ruining them for their intended purpose of cleanly cutting heavy tissue.

instruments.name: Metzenbaum Scissors
instruments.description: Delicate surgical scissors with long shanks and fine blades used exclusively for dissecting and cutting delicate tissues.
instruments.detailedDescription: Metzenbaum scissors (often simply called "Metz") have a distinctively high handle-to-blade ratio, meaning the shanks are long and the cutting blades are relatively short. The blades are thin, elegant, and almost always curved, ending in blunt tips. They are the primary instrument used for delicate blunt and sharp dissection of soft tissues.
instruments.uses: 🩸 Blunt dissection (spreading tissue planes) and sharp cutting of delicate tissues, peritoneum, or adhesions.
instruments.parts:
🔹 Ring handles
🔹 Long, slender shanks
🔹 Box lock joint
🔹 Thin, delicate curved blades with blunt tips
instruments.mechanism: ⚙️ The long shanks provide deep reach into cavities while the short, thin blades offer high precision and mechanical control for fine shearing and blunt spreading.
instruments.commonProcedures:
🩺 Deep orthopedic exposures
🩺 Bowel surgery
🩺 Plastic surgery
instruments.clinicalTip: 💡 The Metzenbaum is a highly refined instrument. Never use it to cut sutures, heavy fascia, drains, or drapes, as this will misalign the delicate blades and destroy their sharp edges.

instruments.name: Iris Scissors
instruments.description: Small, very fine scissors with sharp points originally designed for eye surgery but widely used for delicate dissection.
instruments.detailedDescription: Iris scissors are small, precise instruments featuring very short blades that taper to acutely sharp points. They can be straight or curved. Because of their fine tips and small size, they offer exceptional control for working on tiny anatomical structures or in very superficial, delicate tissue planes.
instruments.uses: 🩸 Fine dissection, precise trimming of tissue edges, and removing fine sutures.
instruments.parts:
🔹 Ring handles
🔹 Short shanks
🔹 Box lock joint
🔹 Fine blades with sharp/sharp tips
instruments.mechanism: ⚙️ A standard shear mechanism scaled down for high-precision, low-force cutting of fragile tissues.
instruments.commonProcedures:
🩺 Hand and foot surgery (Podiatry)
🩺 Ophthalmic surgery
🩺 Plastic and reconstructive surgery
instruments.clinicalTip: 💡 Because the tips are exceptionally sharp and pointy, exercise extreme caution when dissecting blindly or working over vital structures (like nerves or arteries) to prevent accidental puncture.

instruments.name: Tenotomy Scissors
instruments.description: Small, delicate scissors with fine blades and blunt tips used to safely dissect and cut delicate structures like tendons.
instruments.detailedDescription: Tenotomy scissors are similar in size to Iris scissors but are distinguished by their blunt, sometimes slightly flattened, tips. This specific design allows the surgeon to safely slide the closed scissors under a tendon or delicate tissue plane, and bluntly spread or cut without the risk of a sharp tip piercing surrounding blood vessels or nerves.
instruments.uses: 🩸 Delicate blunt dissection, cutting tendons, and working safely near neurovascular bundles.
instruments.parts:
🔹 Ring handles
🔹 Short, delicate shanks
🔹 Box lock joint
🔹 Narrow blades with blunt tips
instruments.mechanism: ⚙️ The blunt tips function as a safe leading edge for probing and spreading tissue planes, followed by a clean shearing action when the blades are engaged.
instruments.commonProcedures:
🩺 Tendon repair (e.g., Achilles, flexor tendons)
🩺 Hand surgery
🩺 Neuroma excisions
instruments.clinicalTip: 💡 Insert the closed blunt tips between tissue layers and gently open the blades. This "spread and cut" technique safely separates structures before you commit to cutting them.

instruments.name: Stevens Scissors
instruments.description: A specific, widely used pattern of tenotomy scissors featuring elongated handles and very short, delicate, tapered blades.
instruments.detailedDescription: Stevens tenotomy scissors are highly favored in fine surgical specialties. They feature notably long shanks compared to their very small, delicate, tapering blades. The blades end in blunt or finely rounded tips. The extended handle length allows the surgeon to maintain a comfortable, stable hand position outside the immediate surgical field while manipulating the tiny blades deep within a wound.
instruments.uses: 🩸 Meticulous dissection of fine tissues, tendons, and neurological structures.
instruments.parts:
🔹 Ring handles
🔹 Elongated shanks
🔹 Box lock joint
🔹 Very short, tapered blades with blunt tips
instruments.mechanism: ⚙️ The long shanks provide excellent reach and visual clearance, while the small blades offer microscopic precision for delicate shearing and spreading.
instruments.commonProcedures:
🩺 Micro-orthopedics (hand/wrist)
🩺 Plastic surgery
🩺 Peripheral nerve decompressions
instruments.clinicalTip: 💡 The Stevens pattern is ideal for "undermining" skin or tissue edges. The blunt tips can safely push along the subcutaneous plane to free up tissue flaps without causing accidental buttonhole punctures in the skin.

instruments.name: Potts Scissors
instruments.description: Fine, long-handled scissors with distinctly angled blades used primarily for precise cutting of blood vessels.
instruments.detailedDescription: Potts scissors (often called Potts-Smith scissors) are easily identified by their long shanks and short blades that are sharply angled—typically at 25, 45, or 60 degrees from the axis of the handle. The tips are usually very sharp. This unique geometry allows the surgeon to insert the tip into a tubular structure (like an artery or vein) and cut longitudinally without their hand or the instrument shaft blocking their line of sight.
instruments.uses: 🩸 Extending incisions in blood vessels (arteriotomy/venotomy) or cutting ducts and nerves.
instruments.parts:
🔹 Ring handles
🔹 Long, slender shanks
🔹 Box lock joint
🔹 Short blades angled at 25°, 45°, or 60°
instruments.mechanism: ⚙️ The angled blades convert a standard horizontal hand squeeze into a vertical or diagonal cut, providing ergonomic access to parallel structures.
instruments.commonProcedures:
🩺 Vascular surgery (AV fistulas, bypass grafts)
🩺 Microsurgical nerve repair
🩺 Artery or vein harvesting
instruments.clinicalTip: 💡 When handing the Potts scissors to the surgeon, ensure the angle of the blade points downwards or in the requested direction of the vessel incision, as the angle dictates the cutting path.

instruments.name: Lister Scissors
instruments.description: Angled, heavy-duty scissors featuring a blunt, flattened nodule on the lower blade used safely remove bandages and dressings.
instruments.detailedDescription: Lister bandage scissors are distinctly angled to allow the user's hand to remain clear of the patient's body while cutting. Their most defining feature is the blunt, flattened, perpendicular nodule (often called a "shoe" or "foot") on the tip of the lower blade. This shoe safely slides under tight bandages, casts, or drapes, lifting the material into the cutting edge without scratching or puncturing the patient's skin.
instruments.uses: 🩸 Cutting wound dressings, surgical drapes, cast padding, and plaster.
instruments.parts:
🔹 Ring handles
🔹 Angled shanks
🔹 Box lock joint
🔹 Upper sharp blade
🔹 Lower blade with blunt, flattened nodule
instruments.mechanism: ⚙️ The blunt shoe glides along the epidermis, displacing the skin away from the sharp upper shearing blade to guarantee patient safety during blind cuts under dressings.
instruments.commonProcedures:
🩺 Post-operative dressing removal
🩺 Cast removal
🩺 Prepping and draping the patient
instruments.clinicalTip: 💡 Always keep the flat "shoe" firmly pressed and parallel against the patient's skin. If you angle the scissors up, you risk catching the skin between the blades.

instruments.name: Littauer Scissors
instruments.description: Specialized scissors with a small hook on one blade used to safely lift and sever surgical sutures.
instruments.detailedDescription: Littauer suture scissors are specifically engineered for the atraumatic removal of stitches. The lower blade features a distinct, small hook at the very tip. This hook is designed to easily slip underneath a tight suture loop, lifting the thread slightly away from the healing skin. The upper blade then drops down to cleanly shear the suture.
instruments.uses: 🩸 Removal of external non-absorbable sutures.
instruments.parts:
🔹 Ring handles
🔹 Box lock joint
🔹 Upper cutting blade
🔹 Lower blade with a specialized distal hook
instruments.mechanism: ⚙️ The hook acts as an anchor to grab and elevate the suture loop, ensuring the scissor cuts only the thread and does not pinch the newly healed skin.
instruments.commonProcedures:
🩺 Post-operative clinical follow-ups
🩺 Suture removal
🩺 Wound checks
instruments.clinicalTip: 💡 When removing a suture, slip the hook under the thread close to the skin and cut. Pull the suture out by the knot to ensure the contaminated portion of the thread resting outside the body is not dragged *through* the healing wound tract.

instruments.name: Suture Scissors
instruments.description: A general term for straight, stout scissors used strictly for cutting suture material and other non-tissue operating room supplies.
instruments.detailedDescription: Suture scissors (often referring to Straight Mayo scissors or Spencer scissors) are the workhorses for non-tissue cutting. They have strong, straight blades that can be blunt/blunt, sharp/blunt, or sharp/sharp. In the operating room, it is a strict rule that suture scissors are used only for cutting inanimate materials to preserve the sharpness of the delicate tissue-dissecting scissors (like Metzenbaums).
instruments.uses: 🩸 Cutting sutures, surgical mesh, vessel loops, and wound drains.
instruments.parts:
🔹 Ring handles
🔹 Box lock joint
🔹 Stout, straight blades
instruments.mechanism: ⚙️ Heavy-duty shearing action capable of cutting through thick, braided, or monofilament synthetic threads without dulling rapidly.
instruments.commonProcedures: 🩺 Utilized in almost every surgical procedure that requires suturing.
instruments.clinicalTip: 💡 When cutting a suture for a surgeon tying a knot, slide the partially open blades down the suture strands to the knot, turn the scissors at a slight 45-degree angle, and cut. This consistently leaves the perfect "tail" length on the knot.

instruments.name: Wire Cutting Scissors
instruments.description: Exceptionally heavy, stout scissors or shears with specialized blades used to cleanly cut surgical wire or metal pins.
instruments.detailedDescription: Wire cutting scissors (or wire shears) are heavily reinforced instruments. They feature very short, thick blades and often have a distinct notch or serration at the base of one or both blades. This notch is critical: it captures the metal wire, preventing it from slipping out toward the tip when heavy pressure is applied. The handles are robust, sometimes resembling pliers rather than standard surgical rings.
instruments.uses: 🩸 Cutting cerclage wire, K-wires, heavy wire sutures, and surgical mesh.
instruments.parts:
🔹 Heavy ring handles or plier grips
🔹 Reinforced, heavy-duty hinge
🔹 Short, thick blades
🔹 Wire-catching notch at the base of the blade
instruments.mechanism: ⚙️ Maximizes mechanical leverage by keeping the cutting point very close to the fulcrum (hinge), providing the immense shear force required to cut through metal wire.
instruments.commonProcedures:
🩺 Open Reduction Internal Fixation (ORIF)
🩺 Sternotomy closure
🩺 Spinal fusion (wiring techniques)
instruments.clinicalTip: 💡 Never use standard tissue, Mayo, or suture scissors to cut wire. The metal wire will instantly notch the stainless steel blades, permanently destroying the instrument's ability to cut anything else.

instruments.name: Bard-Parker Handle No. 3
instruments.description: A standard scalpel handle used for precise, delicate skin and tissue incisions.
instruments.detailedDescription: The Bard-Parker No. 3 is the most frequently used scalpel handle in surgery. It features a ribbed grip and a specialized slotted tip that securely locks small blades into place. It is compatible with smaller surgical blades such as the No. 10, 11, 12, and 15. Its size and weight make it ideal for shallow, precise incisions where fine motor control is paramount.
instruments.uses: 🩸 Creating superficial skin incisions and performing delicate sharp dissection.
instruments.parts:
🔹 Slotted blade-mounting tip
🔹 Ribbed grip handle
🔹 Flat, rigid body
instruments.mechanism: ⚙️ The flat, slotted tip securely engages the corresponding slit in the surgical blade, locking it rigidly to prevent slipping or deflection during precision cutting.
instruments.commonProcedures:
🩺 Excisional biopsies
🩺 Minor orthopedic approaches (e.g., carpal tunnel release)
🩺 Plastic and reconstructive surgery
instruments.clinicalTip: 💡 When loading a blade onto a No. 3 handle, always use a heavy needle holder to grasp the blade just above the slot. Pushing it on with your fingers is a primary cause of accidental sharps injuries in the OR.

instruments.name: Bard-Parker Handle No. 4
instruments.description: A larger, heavier scalpel handle designed to hold large blades for major incisions.
instruments.detailedDescription: The Bard-Parker No. 4 handle has a wider, longer, and thicker body compared to the No. 3. Its larger slotted tip accommodates heavy-duty blades such as the No. 20, 21, 22, 23, and 24. This robust construction provides the surgeon with a firm grip and the mechanical stability needed to cut through thick, dense tissue layers like heavy orthopedic capsules or thick fascia.
instruments.uses: 🩸 Creating long, deep skin incisions and cutting heavy fascial or capsular layers.
instruments.parts:
🔹 Large slotted blade-mounting tip
🔹 Wide ribbed grip handle
🔹 Heavy, flat body
instruments.mechanism: ⚙️ The enlarged slotted tip locks large blades rigidly in place, allowing the surgeon to transfer greater downward cutting force without the blade snapping or deflecting.
instruments.commonProcedures:
🩺 Major orthopedic joint exposures (hips/knees)
🩺 Laparotomy
🩺 Thoracotomy
instruments.clinicalTip: 💡 Use this handle loaded with a No. 20 or 22 blade for the initial thick skin incision in a total joint arthroplasty, where firm pressure is required to cleanly reach the subcutaneous fat layer in one pass.

instruments.name: Bard-Parker Handle No. 7
instruments.description: A long, slender scalpel handle designed for precise cutting in deep, narrow surgical fields.
instruments.detailedDescription: The Bard-Parker No. 7 handle resembles a long writing pen. It is specifically designed to reach deep into confined anatomical spaces where standard flat handles would block the surgeon's line of sight or bump into surrounding retractors. It uses the same small blades as the No. 3 handle (such as No. 10, 11, 12, and 15), providing high precision in tight quarters.
instruments.uses: 🩸 Fine cutting and sharp dissection in deep, restricted anatomical spaces.
instruments.parts:
🔹 Long, slender rounded shaft
🔹 Slotted blade-mounting tip
🔹 Tapered grip
instruments.mechanism: ⚙️ The elongated, slender shaft allows the instrument to function like a precision pen, affording fine control of the cutting tip deep within anatomical cavities.
instruments.commonProcedures:
🩺 Deep joint capsule incisions
🩺 Spinal surgery
🩺 Maxillofacial and ENT procedures
instruments.clinicalTip: 💡 Hold the No. 7 handle using a "pencil grip" to maximize fine motor control when dissecting delicate tissues deep within a wound.

instruments.name: Blade No. 10
instruments.description: A standard small blade with a curved cutting edge used for making traditional skin incisions.
instruments.detailedDescription: The No. 10 blade is one of the most widely used surgical blades. It features a large, curved cutting edge and a flat, unsharpened back. It is designed to be used with a No. 3 or No. 7 handle. It is ideal for making linear, controlled incisions through skin and superficial subcutaneous tissue.
instruments.uses: 🩸 Making initial skin incisions and performing sharp dissection of soft tissues.
instruments.parts:
🔹 Curved cutting edge ("belly")
🔹 Flat, unsharpened back margin
🔹 Slotted base for mounting
instruments.mechanism: ⚙️ Slices cleanly through tissue as the curved belly of the blade is drawn linearly across the skin with moderate downward pressure.
instruments.commonProcedures:
🩺 General orthopedic skin incisions
🩺 Tendon repairs
🩺 General surgery incisions
instruments.clinicalTip: 💡 When using a No. 10 blade, cut with the curved "belly" of the blade rather than dragging just the tip. Using the belly ensures a smooth, even incision depth.

instruments.name: Blade No. 11
instruments.description: A triangular blade with a sharply pointed tip, ideal for stab incisions and precise, short cuts.
instruments.detailedDescription: The No. 11 blade is an elongated, triangular blade with a hypotenuse forming a flat cutting edge and an acutely pointed tip. It mounts on a No. 3 or No. 7 handle. Its sharp point makes it the blade of choice for plunging cuts (stab incisions) to open vessels, drain abscesses, or create small portals for minimally invasive instruments.
instruments.uses: 🩸 Creating stab incisions, opening blood vessels, and initiating minimally invasive portals.
instruments.parts:
🔹 Acutely pointed tip
🔹 Flat, straight cutting edge
🔹 Unsharpened back margin
🔹 Slotted base
instruments.mechanism: ⚙️ Designed to puncture tissue cleanly with its sharp point and then slice via the flat cutting edge as the blade is withdrawn or advanced.
instruments.commonProcedures:
🩺 Arthroscopy (creating portals)
🩺 Incision and drainage (I&D)
🩺 Arteriotomy
instruments.clinicalTip: 💡 When making a stab incision, keep the blade exactly perpendicular to the tissue to prevent "skiving" (making an unwanted beveled, angled cut) through the skin layers.

instruments.name: Blade No. 12
instruments.description: A small, hook-shaped blade with the cutting edge on the inside curve, used for cutting in tight spaces via a pulling motion.
instruments.detailedDescription: The No. 12 blade is uniquely shaped like a small crescent or hook. The sharpened cutting edge is located on the concave (inside) curve of the blade. It fits onto a No. 3 or No. 7 handle and is heavily utilized when the surgeon needs to hook a structure—such as a suture or a duct—and pull the blade toward themselves to cut it safely.
instruments.uses: 🩸 Dissecting behind structures, mucosal procedures, and cutting tight sutures.
instruments.parts:
🔹 Hook-shaped tip
🔹 Concave inner cutting edge
🔹 Slotted base
